messages.content: improve and make it more academic: Regarding autocratic bureaucracy, Mencius often took a transcendent and critical stance, while Xunzi sought to put himself in the place of concrete plans. They both respected the “rule by ritual”, but Mencius particularly advocated the way of teachers, while Xunzi did not abandon “the way of officials”. The two differed in their focus on ritual. The contributions of Xunzi are as follows.

When examining the concept of autocratic bureaucracy, Mencius consistently adopted a lofty and discerning position, whereas Xunzi veered towards practical and tangible approaches. Although they shared an appreciation for "rule by ritual", Mencius prominently endorsed an instructional modus operandi, whereas Xunzi retained a commitment to administrative methods. Furthermore, their perspectives diverged when it came to the importance placed on ritual. The specific contributions of Xunzi can be delineated as follows.

improve and make it more academic:  First, from the perspective of the “transmission of scriptures”, Xunzi was the master of Confucianism at the end of the Warring States Period, and laid the theoretical foundation for the “Great Unity” of imperial politics after the Qin and Han. The Book of Rites, except for the “The Doctrine of the Mean”, basically passed down and developed the thought of Xunzi. Therefore, it has been said that the political and ideological traditions of imperial China were “a combination of Confucianism and law”, “Confucianism on the surface and law in essence”, and that “the learning of two millennia was the doctrine of Xunzi”.

To elucidate the academic significance of Xunzi's contributions, it must first be acknowledged that he emerged as the foremost Confucian scholar during the concluding period of the Warring States. His profound insights laid the conceptual groundwork for the subsequent establishment of imperial political harmony, which ensued during the Qin and Han dynasties. Notably, the influence of Xunzi's perspectives was discernible in significant portions of the Book of Rites, with the sole exception being "The Doctrine of the Mean". This comprehensive text substantially fostered the development and propagation of Xunzi's philosophical doctrines. Consequently, scholars assert that the sociopolitical and ideological traditions that permeated imperial China can be perceived as a nuanced amalgamation of Confucian values and legal principles. It has even been posited that the quintessential essence of two millennia's worth of erudition bore the unmistakable imprint of Xunzi's doctrines.

improve and make it more academic: Second, during the Spring and Autumn Period and the Warring States Period, the Zhou’s political and cultural traditions of rule by ritual were developed in different directions by the masters, and “The system of the Dao was about to be torn in fragments all under the sky”.  Xunzi therein was less paranoid and more comprehensive, and made a creative transformation of the ritual rule of the trinity of politics, kin and culture, thus preparing a theoretical ground for the inheritance and advancement of ancient Chinese scholar-bureaucratic politics.

Another significant contribution of Xunzi lies in his response to the divergence and potential disintegration of the Zhou dynasty's political and cultural traditions during the Spring and Autumn Period and the Warring States Period. The fragmentation of the ritual-based governance system loomed as a pressing concern, engendering instability and discord across the realm. In contrast to his contemporaries, Xunzi demonstrated a judicious and comprehensive approach, transcending any paranoiac sentiment. He embarked upon a transformative endeavor to reconceptualize and revitalize the ritualistic governance model, encompassing the interplay between the realms of politics, kinship, and culture. Consequently, this intellectual and practical reimagining laid a solid theoretical foundation for the continued prominence and advancement of the ancient Chinese scholar-bureaucratic political tradition.

improve and make it more academic:  Thirdly, his mode of “scholar-gentlemen—officials and functionaries” carried on the gentry politics in the enfeoffment era and pioneered the mode of “scholar-bureaucrat—clerks” in the imperial era.

Another noteworthy aspect of Xunzi's contributions pertains to his distinctive approach in the sphere of governance. He championed a model known as "scholar-gentlemen—officials and functionaries," which not only upheld the principles of gentry politics prevalent during the enfeoffment era but also played a pioneering role in shaping the subsequent mode of "scholar-bureaucrat—clerks" within the imperial era.

The encapsulation of Xunzi's vision can be construed as an amalgamation of two distinct yet interconnected elements. Firstly, he recognized the significance of the scholar-gentleman archetype, which aligned with the societal and political ideals of the enfeoffment era. This model held revered positions within the scholarly gentry class, seamlessly integrating intellectual prowess and political authority. Secondly, Xunzi's foresight extended beyond the enfeoffment era, pioneering the emergence of an evolved bureaucratic structure within the imperial era. In this paradigm, the scholar-bureaucrat assumed a prominent role, harnessing their erudition and administrative acumen to effectively discharge their duties. Supporting their endeavors were the clerks, who provided indispensable administrative support, thereby facilitating the smooth functioning of the imperial apparatus.

Through his innovative conceptualization, Xunzi navigated the complex realms of gentry politics and imperial bureaucracy, ensuring continuity while adapting to the evolving political milieu. This enduring legacy reflects his profound impact on the development of governance structures in ancient China.

improve and make it more academic: Musician officials and historiographical officials were the main carriers of ancient culture. Music teachers’ responsibility for ritual and education gave rise to the “Way of the Teacher” while historiographers’ responsibility for historical records and law advanced the “Way of the Official”. The evolution of historiographical officials of the Zhou constitutes the origin and development of the later scholar-officials. The historiographers in charge of “historical records and law undoubtedly played a catalytic role in the standardization, routinization, documentation, and legalization of administration.” Musician officials taught scholars and were responsible for rituals and teaching, thus less involved in the specific administration of “war, penalty, money and grain” (Yan, 2001). Compared with the scholar-bureaucratic group, clerical officials had three characteristics: first, specialized training in administrative skills; second, strict compliance with laws and regulations and occupied by paperwork; third, promotion according to ability, merits and years of service. The dual-role scholar-bureaucratic politics did not emerge until the Han Dynasty, and the relationship between scholar-masters and administrative clerks had gone through several stages of separation, opposition, integration and division.

Musician officials and historiographical officials held prominent roles in preserving and transmitting ancient cultural practices. The former, responsible for ritual and educational matters, fostered the emergence of the "Way of the Teacher," while the latter, tasked with historical documentation and legal affairs, contributed to the advancement of the "Way of the Official." The evolution of historiographical officials during the Zhou dynasty serves as the foundational framework for the subsequent development of scholar-officials. Notably, the pioneering efforts of historiographers in recording historical events and codifying legal principles played an instrumental role in the standardization, systematization, archival documentation, and institutionalization of administrative practices.

Musician officials primarily focused on instructing scholars and carrying out ritualistic duties, thereby being less directly involved in the specific administration of warfare, legal penalties, financial matters, and food distribution (Yan, 2001). In contrast, clerical officials possessed distinct characteristics within the scholar-bureaucratic framework. These traits encompassed specialized training in administrative skills, strict adherence to laws and regulations, and an occupation primarily centered on paperwork. Furthermore, these officials endured advancements in their careers based on their abilities, merits, and years of service.

The emergence of the dual-role scholar-bureaucratic governance system did not materialize until the Han Dynasty. The dynamic relationship between scholar-masters and administrative clerks underwent several stages characterized by periods of separation, opposition, integration, and division. Such complex processes shaped the intricate dynamics and structures inherent in ancient Chinese governance.

improve and make it more academic:  In the late Spring and Autumn Period and the Warring States Period, the “scholar class”, including scholar-master (specialists), strategists (coalition advisors), necromancer or alchemists, and retainers, was active. The large-scale reform movement during the Warring States Period can be regarded as the social transformation of authoritarian bureaucratization, among which the state of Qin was a typical bureaucratic empire and had a professional bureaucratic group comprised of “clerical officials” separated from “scholar-masters”.

During the latter stages of the Spring and Autumn Period and the Warring States Period, a notable entity known as the "scholar class" occupied a significant role in political affairs. This class encompassed various subgroups, such as scholar-masters (specialists), strategists (coalition advisors), necromancers, alchemists, and retainers, who actively participated in societal functions. Notably, the comprehensive reform movement that unfolded during the Warring States Period can be construed as an extensive sociopolitical transformation towards autocratic bureaucracy.

In this transformative era, the State of Qin surfaced as a quintessential example of a bureaucratic empire, characterized by the establishment and operation of a professional bureaucratic cadre known as "clerical officials." These officials were distinct from the scholar-masters, marking a clear separation of roles and functions within the bureaucracy.

The emergence of the bureaucratic empire under the State of Qin epitomized a shift towards a centralized and specialized administrative system. It demonstrated the process of bureaucratic professionalization, as clerical officials assumed responsibilities traditionally undertaken by scholar-masters. This development contributed further to the evolution of autocratic bureaucracy during this period.

improve and make it more academic:  The Qin Dynasty pursued the legalists’ idea of promoting law by learning from “clerical officials” and “non-academic doctrine”, treated scholar-masters as “pests”, “burned books and buried Confucian scholars”, showed a contempt toward culture, and adopted cultural autocracy. In the “worship of order and philosophy of power” of legalism, moral perfection and moral values, cultural development and academic discussions were rendered meaningless. Officials were merely cogs and screws of the bureaucratic machine while the common people were merely personified servitude and taxation; they were only meaningful in terms of numbers and functions” (Yan, 1996, p. 175). It can be said that, in a certain sense, it was the theory of legalism and the limitations of official politics that led to the downfall of the Qin Empire.

The Qin Dynasty notably adhered to legalist ideologies, which emphasized the paramountcy of law and administration over other societal aspects. This ideological framework disregarded the significance of cultural and intellectual pursuits, resulting in the suppression of scholar-masters and the destruction of valuable texts through book burnings and censorship. The Qin rulers, perceiving scholars as undesirables, undermined the importance of cultural traditions and manifested a disdain for intellectual discourse.

The Qin Dynasty can be characterized by its adoption of a model of cultural autocracy, wherein the promotion of law and administrative control took precedence. This legalistic perspective fostered an environment where moral excellence, cultural development, and academic deliberations held little value. The bureaucracy operated as a rigid machine, with officials merely functioning as impersonal gears, while the common people were reduced to mere objects of taxation and servitude, devoid of intrinsic worth beyond their assigned roles and numerical contributions (Yan, 1996, p. 175).

In retrospect, it can be argued that the philosophical underpinnings of legalism, coupled with the limitations inherent in the prevailing system of official politics, ultimately played a significant role in the downfall of the Qin Empire. The prioritization of order and power, at the expense of moral and cultural considerations, proved to be a fundamental flaw that contributed to the empire's decline.

improve and make it more academic: After a period of separation and opposition, scholar-masters and clerical officials merged into the scholar-bureaucrat class in the Han Dynasty when the officials were selected from clerical officials on the level of commandery and district, and Confucian scholars often depended on the steppingstone of “government clerks” in their official career. “Confucian scholars were fully bureaucratized”, and “in general, all officials were clerks” (e.g., the “Ministry of Personnel” was actually in charge of all officials). The Han selected candidates for state offices according to the disciplines of virtue, Classics, law, and political affairs, either from Confucian students based on moral integrity or clerks based on, working ability. The commandery quota system, which recruited officials of filiality and incorruptibility, was based on the spirit of “ritual rule” of “talent selection by virtue”. The Eastern Han corrected the shortcomings of Wang Mang’s “New Deal” (AD 8) by evaluating “filial and incorrupt” officials on their “political competence and achievements as the basis for promotion” and “selecting talent by their capability”, which greatly promoted the integration of Confucian scholars and clerical officials (Yan, 1996, pp. 13-15).

In the Han Dynasty, scholar-masters and clerical officials, previously perceived as separate entities, underwent a process of amalgamation, resulting in the emergence of the scholar-bureaucrat class. This transformation occurred during the selection process for officials, wherein candidates were primarily chosen from among the existing pool of clerical officials at the commandery and district levels. Confucian scholars often relied on beginning their official careers as "government clerks," enabling them to ascend the bureaucratic ladder. Consequently, it can be asserted that Confucian scholars became fully integrated into the bureaucracy, while officials, in general, predominantly held positions as clerks. Notably, the Ministry of Personnel, despite its label, held jurisdiction over all officials, highlighting the dominance of the bureaucratic system.

During the Han Dynasty, candidates for state offices were selected based on their aptitude in virtue, knowledge of Classics, understanding of the law, and proficiency in political affairs. Selection was contingent upon candidates' moral integrity if they were Confucian students or based on their working abilities if they belonged to the clerical officials. The commandery quota system, designed to recruit officials known for their filial piety and incorruptibility, adhered to the principles of the "ritual rule" wherein talent selection was based on virtue. This system emphasized the importance of moral character alongside administrative competence.

The Eastern Han Dynasty rectified the deficiencies of Wang Mang's "New Deal" in AD 8 by adopting an evaluation system that assessed officials for their political competence, achievements, and capability, in addition to their filial piety and integrity. This approach significantly facilitated the integration of Confucian scholars and clerical officials within the system, promoting a more comprehensive and merit-based selection process (Yan, 1996, pp. 13-15).

improve and make it more academic: However, in the minds of the people then, there was a clear difference between Confucian scholars and clerks: “Clerks are good at running routine work but lack in loyalty; Confucian scholars boast moral integrity but are bad at handling affairs. Both have strengths and weaknesses and each has their own suitable job ... Recruitment from Confucian scholars should be assessed according to morality and virtue, while that from clerks, capability in running office work … What Confucian students learn is the Way (dao,道, principle) while what clerks learn is how to deal with specific matters … Confucian scholars deal with the essentials while clerks, the details. Comparing the essential principles with the specific details, it is obvious to tell superiority and inferiority in talent selection.” (Discursive Weighing · Weighing of Talents) During the reign of Emperor Shun of the Eastern Han, the elevation of filial and incorrupt persons shifted its focus to “literary competence”, which continued through the Wei, Jin, and Northern and Southern Dynasties, and evolved into the imperial examination system during the Sui and Tang dynasties. The gentry class was responsible for maintaining the political and cultural mode while clerical officials, for technical and administrative affairs (Yan, 1996, pp. 15-16).

Within the societal context of that era, a clear distinction existed in people's perceptions between Confucian scholars and clerks. The prevailing sentiment was that clerks excelled in executing routine tasks but lacked loyalty, while Confucian scholars prided themselves on moral integrity but faced challenges in handling practical affairs. Each group possessed its respective strengths and weaknesses, making them more suitable for specific roles. Consequently, recruitment from Confucian scholars was often evaluated based on morality and virtue, whereas the capabilities of clerks in managing office work were emphasized.

Confucian scholars primarily focused on learning the fundamental principles, or the "Way" (dao), while clerks acquired expertise in dealing with specific and nuanced matters. Confucian scholars dealt with essential aspects, while clerks attended to intricacies and details. Comparing the overarching principles with the specific details, it becomes evident how talent selection can vary in terms of superiority and inferiority.

During the reign of Emperor Shun in the Eastern Han Dynasty, there was a shifting emphasis towards the elevation of individuals distinguished by their filial piety and moral incorruptibility. This focus gradually transformed into a greater emphasis on "literary competence" that persisted through subsequent dynasties such as Wei, Jin, and the Northern and Southern Dynasties. Eventually, this emphasis on literary competence evolved into the imperial examination system during the Sui and Tang dynasties. In this evolving landscape, the gentry class assumed responsibility for upholding the political and cultural norms, while clerical officials specialized in technical and administrative affairs (Yan, 1996, pp. 15-16).
